front: What is the core assumption of Naive Bayes classifiers regarding features?
back: The Naive Bayes assumption is that features are conditionally independent given the class label. This means the presence (or absence) of a particular feature is unrelated to the presence (or absence) of any other feature, given the class variable.

front: What is the formula for the posterior probability P(Y | X) used in Naive Bayes classification?
back: P(Y | X) = ( P(Y) * P(X | Y) ) / P(X). Using the Naive assumption, this simplifies to: P(Y | X₁, X₂, ..., Xₙ) ∝ P(Y) * Πᵢ P(Xᵢ | Y), where we calculate the product of the prior and all the individual feature probabilities given the class.

front: What is the purpose of applying Laplace (additive) smoothing in a Naive Bayes model?
back: Laplace smoothing is used to handle zero probability events for categorical features. It adds a small value (often 1) to the count of each feature for every class, preventing a probability of zero for any P(Xᵢ | Y), which would otherwise cause the entire posterior probability product to become zero.

front: How does a Gaussian Naive Bayes model differ from the standard (Multinomial) version?
back: Gaussian Naive Bayes is used for continuous-valued features. Instead of using frequency counts, it assumes that the likelihood of each feature given a class, P(Xᵢ | Y), follows a Gaussian (normal) distribution. It uses the mean and variance of the feature values within each class to calculate probabilities.

front: What are the key advantages of the Naive Bayes classification algorithm?
back:
1. It is simple, fast, and efficient, even for large datasets.
2. It performs well with high-dimensional data (many features).
3. It can be very effective even when the naive independence assumption is violated in practice.
